examples.explanation: Step 1: Initialize result = []. Step 2: i=0, word="bat". j=0: skip (i=j). j=1: combined="bat"+"tab"="battab", check palindrome: "battab" reversed is "battab" → palindrome → add [0,1]. j=2: combined="bat"+"cat"="batcat", check: "batcat" reversed "tactab" → not palindrome. Step 3: i=1, word="tab". j=0: combined="tab"+"bat"="tabbat", check: "tabbat" reversed "tabbat" → palindrome → add [1,0]. j=1: skip. j=2: combined="tab"+"cat"="tabcat", check: "tabcat" reversed "tactab" → not palindrome. Step 4: i=2, word="cat". j=0: combined="cat"+"bat"="catbat", check: "catbat" reversed "tabtac" → not palindrome. j=1: combined="cat"+"tab"="cattab", check: "cattab" reversed "battac" → not palindrome. j=2: skip. Final result: [[0,1], [1,0]].

Step 1: result = []. Step 2: i=0, word="a". j=0: skip. j=1: combined="ab", check: "ab" reversed "ba" → not palindrome. j=2: combined="ac", check: "ac" reversed "ca" → not palindrome. j=3: combined="aaa", check: "aaa" reversed "aaa" → palindrome → add [0,3]. Step 3: i=1, word="b". j=0: combined="ba", check: "ba" reversed "ab" → not palindrome. j=1: skip. j=2: combined="bc", check: "bc" reversed "cb" → not palindrome. j=3: combined="baa", check: "baa" reversed "aab" → not palindrome. Step 4: i=2, word="c". j=0: combined="ca", check: "ca" reversed "ac" → not palindrome. j=1: combined="cb", check: "cb" reversed "bc" → not palindrome. j=2: skip. j=3: combined="caa", check: "caa" reversed "aac" → not palindrome. Step 5: i=3, word="aa". j=0: combined="aaa", check: "aaa" reversed "aaa" → palindrome → add [3,0]. j=1: combined="aab", check: "aab" reversed "baa" → not palindrome. j=2: combined="aac", check: "aac" reversed "caa" → not palindrome. j=3: skip. Final result: [[0,3], [3,0]].

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n² * k)
approaches.summary: Check all possible pairs of words by concatenating them and verifying if the resulting string is a palindrome. This is straightforward but inefficient for large inputs.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Palindrome Pairs
ladders.desc: Find all index pairs (i, j) where concatenating words[i] + words[j] forms a palindrome.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete brute force solution to the palindrome pairs problem. The intuition is straightforward: we need to check every possible pair of words in the input list, concatenate them in both possible orders (i,j and j,i), and verify if the resulting string reads the same forwards and backwards. This works because we're exhaustively exploring all possible combinations - if a palindrome pair exists, we'll find it by checking all pairs. The algorithm: 1) Initialize an empty result list. 2) Use nested loops: outer loop i from 0 to n-1, inner loop j from 0 to n-1. 3) Skip when i == j (same word). 4) Concatenate words[i] + words[j]. 5) Check if concatenated string is palindrome. 6) If yes, add (i,j) to result. Time complexity is O(n² * k) where n is number of words and k is average word length (for palindrome check). Space complexity is O(1) excluding output storage. This connects to the parent problem by being the most naive but correct solution. Common mistakes include: forgetting to check both orders (i,j and j,i are different), including same-index pairs, inefficient palindrome checking. Real code pattern: def palindromePairs(words): result = []; for i in range(len(words)): for j in range(len(words)): if i != j: combined = words[i] + words[j]; if combined == combined[::-1]: result.append([i, j]); return result.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize result = []. Step 2: i=0, word="bat". j=0: skip (i=j). j=1: combined="bat"+"tab"="battab", check palindrome: "battab" reversed is "battab" → palindrome → add [0,1]. j=2: combined="bat"+"cat"="batcat", check: "batcat" reversed "tactab" → not palindrome. Step 3: i=1, word="tab". j=0: combined="tab"+"bat"="tabbat", check: "tabbat" reversed "tabbat" → palindrome → add [1,0]. j=1: skip. j=2: combined="tab"+"cat"="tabcat", check: "tabcat" reversed "tactab" → not palindrome. Step 4: i=2, word="cat". j=0: combined="cat"+"bat"="catbat", check: "catbat" reversed "tabtac" → not palindrome. j=1: combined="cat"+"tab"="cattab", check: "cattab" reversed "battac" → not palindrome. j=2: skip. Final result: [[0,1], [1,0]].

Step 1: result = []. Step 2: i=0, word="a". j=0: skip. j=1: combined="ab", check: "ab" reversed "ba" → not palindrome. j=2: combined="ac", check: "ac" reversed "ca" → not palindrome. j=3: combined="aaa", check: "aaa" reversed "aaa" → palindrome → add [0,3]. Step 3: i=1, word="b". j=0: combined="ba", check: "ba" reversed "ab" → not palindrome. j=1: skip. j=2: combined="bc", check: "bc" reversed "cb" → not palindrome. j=3: combined="baa", check: "baa" reversed "aab" → not palindrome. Step 4: i=2, word="c". j=0: combined="ca", check: "ca" reversed "ac" → not palindrome. j=1: combined="cb", check: "cb" reversed "bc" → not palindrome. j=2: skip. j=3: combined="caa", check: "caa" reversed "aac" → not palindrome. Step 5: i=3, word="aa". j=0: combined="aaa", check: "aaa" reversed "aaa" → palindrome → add [3,0]. j=1: combined="aab", check: "aab" reversed "baa" → not palindrome. j=2: combined="aac", check: "aac" reversed "caa" → not palindrome. j=3: skip. Final result: [[0,3], [3,0]].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Palindrome Check for Concatenated Strings
ladders.desc: Given two strings, concatenate them and determine if the result is a palindrome.
ladders.explanation: This is the most important helper function in the brute force approach. The intuition is that we need to verify if a string reads the same forwards and backwards after concatenation. This works by: 1) Concatenating the two input strings in the given order. 2) Checking the resulting string character by character from both ends. The algorithm: 1) Combine str1 and str2 into a single string s. 2) Initialize left = 0, right = len(s)-1. 3) While left < right: if s[left] != s[right], return False. 4) Increment left, decrement right. 5) If loop completes, return True. Time complexity is O(k) where k is total length of concatenated string. Space complexity is O(k) for the concatenated string (could be O(1) with pointer approach). This feeds into the parent problem by being called for every pair of words. Common mistakes include: not handling empty strings correctly, off-by-one errors in indices, inefficient string concatenation. Real code pattern: def isPalindromePair(str1, str2): s = str1 + str2; left, right = 0, len(s)-1; while left < right: if s[left] != s[right]: return False; left += 1; right -= 1; return True.

examples.trace: Step 1: Concatenate: s = "bat" + "tab" = "battab". Step 2: left=0, right=5. Step 3: s[0]='b', s[5]='b' → equal. Step 4: left=1, right=4. Step 5: s[1]='a', s[4]='a' → equal. Step 6: left=2, right=3. Step 7: s[2]='t', s[3]='t' → equal. Step 8: left=3, right=2 → loop ends (left >= right). Step 9: Return True.
Step 1: Concatenate: s = "hello" + "world" = "helloworld". Step 2: left=0, right=9. Step 3: s[0]='h', s[9]='d' → not equal. Step 4: Return False immediately.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Nested Loop Pair Generation
ladders.desc: Generate all possible ordered pairs of indices from a list, excluding same-index pairs.
ladders.explanation: This is the core logic of the brute force approach: generating all possible (i, j) pairs where i and j are indices in the words list, and i ≠ j. The intuition is that we need to consider every word as a potential first element and every other word (including itself in different position) as potential second element. This works because for palindrome checking, order matters - "ab" + "ba" is different from "ba" + "ab". The algorithm: 1) Get n = length of words list. 2) For i from 0 to n-1: 3) For j from 0 to n-1: 4) If i != j, process pair (i, j). Time complexity is O(n²) for pair generation. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to level above by providing the pairs to check for palindrome property. Common mistakes include: using range that excludes endpoints, forgetting to exclude i=j, misunderstanding that (i,j) and (j,i) are different pairs. Real code pattern: n = len(words); for i in range(n): for j in range(n): if i != j: # Process words[i] and words[j].

examples.trace: Step 1: n=3. Step 2: i=0: j=0 skip (i=j), j=1 process (0,1), j=2 process (0,2). Step 3: i=1: j=0 process (1,0), j=1 skip, j=2 process (1,2). Step 4: i=2: j=0 process (2,0), j=1 process (2,1), j=2 skip. Total 6 pairs.
Step 1: n=1. Step 2: i=0: j=0 skip (i=j). No pairs generated.

ladders.title: Building Block — String Concatenation
ladders.desc: Combine two strings into a single string in a specific order.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept is essential for the brute force approach: we need to combine two words to check if their concatenation forms a palindrome. The intuition is simple - we're creating a new string by putting the first string immediately followed by the second string. This works because palindrome checking requires examining the complete combined string. The algorithm: 1) Take two input strings str1 and str2. 2) Create a new string result = str1 + str2. 3) Return result. Time complexity is O(m+n) where m,n are lengths of input strings (creating new string). Space complexity is O(m+n) for the new string. This connects to level above by providing the concatenated string for palindrome checking. Common mistakes include: modifying original strings, inefficient concatenation in loops, not preserving order. Real code pattern: def concatenate(str1, str2): return str1 + str2.

examples.trace: Step 1: Take str1 = "hello". Step 2: Take str2 = "world". Step 3: Create new string: "hello" + "world" = "helloworld". Step 4: Return "helloworld".
Step 1: str1 = "" (empty string). Step 2: str2 = "test". Step 3: Concatenate: "" + "test" = "test". Step 4: Return "test".

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Character Comparison in String
ladders.desc: Compare characters at specific positions in a string.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation is used in palindrome checking: comparing characters from opposite ends of a string. The intuition is that for a string to be palindrome, the first character must equal the last, second must equal second-last, etc. This works by accessing string indices and using equality comparison. The algorithm: 1) Given string s and indices i, j. 2) Access s[i] and s[j] using array indexing. 3) Compare using equality operator (==). 4) Return True if equal, False otherwise. Time complexity is O(1). Space complexity is O(1). This connects to level above by being the core operation in palindrome checking loop. Common mistakes include: index out of bounds, confusing 0-based indexing, not handling empty strings. Real code pattern: def compareChars(s, i, j): return s[i] == s[j].

examples.trace: Step 1: s = "racecar". Step 2: s[0] = 'r'. Step 3: s[6] = 'r'. Step 4: Compare 'r' == 'r' → True.
Step 1: s = "hello". Step 2: s[1] = 'e'. Step 3: s[3] = 'l'. Step 4: Compare 'e' == 'l' → False.

ladders.title: L5: Concept Foundation — What is a Palindrome?
ladders.desc: A string that reads the same forwards and backwards.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: understanding what a palindrome is. A palindrome is a sequence (in this case, a string) that reads the same forwards and backwards. The intuition is symmetry - the string has mirror symmetry around its center. This works because we can verify it by comparing characters from both ends moving inward. Examples: "racecar", "madam", "a", "" (empty string is palindrome). The algorithm to check: 1) Start with two pointers: left at beginning, right at end. 2) While left < right: compare characters at these positions. 3) If any pair doesn't match, it's not a palindrome. 4) If all match, it is a palindrome. Time complexity is O(n) for string of length n. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to level above as the fundamental property we're checking for. Common mistakes include: thinking single character isn't palindrome, forgetting empty string is palindrome, case sensitivity issues. Real code pattern: def isPalindrome(s): left, right = 0, len(s)-1; while left < right: if s[left] != s[right]: return False; left += 1; right -= 1; return True.

examples.trace: Step 1: left=0 ('r'), right=6 ('r') → equal. Step 2: left=1 ('a'), right=5 ('a') → equal. Step 3: left=2 ('c'), right=4 ('c') → equal. Step 4: left=3 ('e'), right=3 → loop ends. Step 5: All comparisons equal → True.
Step 1: left=0 ('h'), right=4 ('o') → not equal. Step 2: Return False immediately.

approaches.name: HashMap O(n * k²)
approaches.summary: Use a hashmap to store words and their indices, then for each word, check all possible prefixes and suffixes to find complementary words that form a palindrome when concatenated. This avoids checking all pairs explicitly.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find All Palindrome Pairs Using HashMap
ladders.desc: Given a list of unique words, find all index pairs (i, j) such that the concatenation of words[i] + words[j] is a palindrome.
ladders.explanation: The intuition behind this approach is to avoid the naive O(n² * k) solution that checks all pairs. Instead, we use a hashmap to store each word and its index for O(1) lookups. For each word, we consider all possible splits into prefix and suffix. If the prefix is a palindrome, we check if the reverse of the suffix exists in the hashmap (and isn't the current word). This would form a palindrome when concatenated: reverse(suffix) + word. Similarly, if the suffix is a palindrome, we check if the reverse of the prefix exists. This covers all cases where two words concatenate to form a palindrome. The algorithm: 1) Build a hashmap of word to index. 2) For each word at index i: a) For each split point j from 0 to len(word): i) Let prefix = word[0:j], suffix = word[j:]. ii) If prefix is palindrome and reverse(suffix) exists in hashmap at index k (k ≠ i), add (k, i). iii) If suffix is palindrome and reverse(prefix) exists in hashmap at index k (k ≠ i), add (i, k). 3) Return all unique pairs. Time: O(n * k²) where n is number of words, k is max length. We iterate n words, for each we check k splits, and each palindrome check is O(k). Space: O(n) for hashmap. Connection to L1: This uses the palindrome checking helper from L1. Common mistakes: Not handling empty strings correctly (empty prefix/suffix are palindromes), adding duplicate pairs, or missing the case where a word is its own reverse. Real code pattern: def palindromePairs(words): word_map = {word: i for i, word in enumerate(words)}; result = []; for i, word in enumerate(words): for j in range(len(word)+1): prefix, suffix = word[:j], word[j:]; if prefix == prefix[::-1]: rev = suffix[::-1]; if rev in word_map and word_map[rev] != i: result.append([word_map[rev], i]); if suffix == suffix[::-1] and j != len(word): rev = prefix[::-1]; if rev in word_map and word_map[rev] != i: result.append([i, word_map[rev]]); return result

examples.trace: Step 1: Build hashmap: {"abcd":0, "dcba":1, "lls":2, "s":3, "sssll":4}. Step 2: Process "abcd" (index 0): j=0: prefix="", suffix="abcd". prefix palindrome? Yes (empty). reverse(suffix)="dcba" exists at index 1, add [1,0]. suffix palindrome? No. j=1: prefix="a", suffix="bcd". prefix palindrome? Yes. reverse(suffix)="dcb" not in map. suffix palindrome? No. Continue j=2,3,4: no more matches. Step 3: Process "dcba" (index 1): j=0: prefix="", suffix="dcba". prefix palindrome? Yes. reverse(suffix)="abcd" exists at index 0, add [0,1]. Step 4: Process "lls" (index 2): j=0: prefix="", suffix="lls". prefix palindrome? Yes. reverse(suffix)="sll" not in map. j=1: prefix="l", suffix="ls". prefix palindrome? Yes. reverse(suffix)="sl" not in map. j=2: prefix="ll", suffix="s". prefix palindrome? Yes. reverse(suffix)="s" exists at index 3, add [3,2]. suffix palindrome? Yes ("s"). reverse(prefix)="ll" not in map. j=3: prefix="lls", suffix="". prefix palindrome? No. suffix palindrome? Yes (empty). reverse(prefix)="sll" not in map. Step 5: Process "s" (index 3): j=0: prefix="", suffix="s". prefix palindrome? Yes. reverse(suffix)="s" exists but same index, skip. suffix palindrome? Yes. reverse(prefix)="" not in map (empty string not in words). j=1: prefix="s", suffix="". prefix palindrome? Yes. reverse(suffix)="" not in map. suffix palindrome? Yes. reverse(prefix)="s" exists but same index. Step 6: Process "sssll" (index 4): j=0: prefix="", suffix="sssll". prefix palindrome? Yes. reverse(suffix)="llsss" not in map. j=1: prefix="s", suffix="ssll". prefix palindrome? Yes. reverse(suffix)="llss" not in map. j=2: prefix="ss", suffix="sll". prefix palindrome? Yes. reverse(suffix)="lls" exists at index 2, add [2,4]. Continue other j: no more. Final result: [[1,0],[0,1],[3,2],[2,4]].

Step 1: Build hashmap: {"bat":0, "tab":1, "cat":2}. Step 2: Process "bat" (index 0): j=0: prefix="", suffix="bat". prefix palindrome? Yes. reverse(suffix)="tab" exists at index 1, add [1,0]. j=1: prefix="b", suffix="at". prefix palindrome? Yes. reverse(suffix)="ta" not in map. j=2: prefix="ba", suffix="t". prefix palindrome? No. suffix palindrome? Yes. reverse(prefix)="ab" not in map. j=3: prefix="bat", suffix="". prefix palindrome? No. suffix palindrome? Yes. reverse(prefix)="tab" exists but same as j=0 case (already added). Step 3: Process "tab" (index 1): j=0: prefix="", suffix="tab". prefix palindrome? Yes. reverse(suffix)="bat" exists at index 0, add [0,1]. Step 4: Process "cat" (index 2): No matches. Final result: [[1,0],[0,1]].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Check if a String is a Palindrome
ladders.desc: Given a string, determine if it reads the same forwards and backwards.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that a palindrome is symmetric around its center. We can check this by comparing characters from both ends moving inward. The algorithm: Use two pointers, left at start and right at end. While left < right, if characters at these positions are not equal, return false. Otherwise, move left rightwards and right leftwards. If loop completes, return true. Time: O(k) where k is string length. Space: O(1). Connection to L0: This is used repeatedly in L0 to check if prefixes/suffixes are palindromes. Common mistakes: Not handling empty string (which is a palindrome), off-by-one errors in indices, or using unnecessary extra memory. Real code pattern: def isPalindrome(s): left, right = 0, len(s)-1; while left < right: if s[left] != s[right]: return False; left += 1; right -= 1; return True

examples.trace: Step 1: left=0 ("r"), right=6 ("r"), equal. Step 2: left=1 ("a"), right=5 ("a"), equal. Step 3: left=2 ("c"), right=4 ("c"), equal. Step 4: left=3, right=3, loop ends (left < right false). Return true.
Step 1: left=0 ("h"), right=4 ("o"), not equal. Return false immediately.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Finding Complementary Words via Prefix/Suffix Splits
ladders.desc: For a given word, find all other words that can concatenate with it to form a palindrome by checking splits.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that for two words A and B to form palindrome A+B, either: 1) B is reverse of A's suffix and A's prefix is palindrome, or 2) B is reverse of A's prefix and A's suffix is palindrome. This covers all cases because the palindrome center must lie within one word. Algorithm: For word w, iterate split point j from 0 to len(w). Let prefix = w[0:j], suffix = w[j:]. If prefix is palindrome, then reverse(suffix) is a candidate for B. If suffix is palindrome, then reverse(prefix) is a candidate for B. Check if these candidates exist in a pre-built hashmap. Time: O(k²) per word (k splits, each palindrome check O(k)). Space: O(1) extra. Connection to L0: This is the main logic applied to each word in L0. Common mistakes: Missing the case where prefix or suffix is empty (both are palindromes), or not handling duplicate words correctly. Real code pattern: for j in range(len(word)+1): prefix, suffix = word[:j], word[j:]; if isPalindrome(prefix): candidate = reverse(suffix); check if candidate in map; if isPalindrome(suffix): candidate = reverse(prefix); check if candidate in map

examples.output: pairs: (0,?) for suffix "s" when prefix "ll" is palindrome
examples.trace: Step 1: j=0: prefix="", suffix="lls". prefix palindrome? Yes. reverse(suffix)="sll" not in map. suffix palindrome? No. Step 2: j=1: prefix="l", suffix="ls". prefix palindrome? Yes. reverse(suffix)="sl" not in map. suffix palindrome? No. Step 3: j=2: prefix="ll", suffix="s". prefix palindrome? Yes. reverse(suffix)="s" exists at index 0, so pair (0, current_index) if current_index is not 0. suffix palindrome? Yes. reverse(prefix)="ll" exists at index 1, so pair (current_index, 1). Step 4: j=3: prefix="lls", suffix="". prefix palindrome? No. suffix palindrome? Yes. reverse(prefix)="sll" not in map.

examples.output: pairs: (0,?) for suffix "bat" when prefix "" is palindrome
examples.trace: Step 1: j=0: prefix="", suffix="bat". prefix palindrome? Yes. reverse(suffix)="tab" exists at index 0, so pair (0, current_index). suffix palindrome? No. Step 2: j=1: prefix="b", suffix="at". prefix palindrome? Yes. reverse(suffix)="ta" not in map. suffix palindrome? No. Step 3: j=2: prefix="ba", suffix="t". prefix palindrome? No. suffix palindrome? Yes. reverse(prefix)="ab" not in map. Step 4: j=3: prefix="bat", suffix="". prefix palindrome? No. suffix palindrome? Yes. reverse(prefix)="tab" exists but same as j=0.

testCases.expected: [(0,current)] for prefix="" and reverse(suffix)="a" exists at 1? But current index might be 1, so skip if same.
[(0,current)] for prefix="" and reverse(suffix)="aba" not in map, etc.
[(0,current)] for prefix="ll" palindrome and reverse(suffix)="s" exists

ladders.title: Building Block — Reverse a String and HashMap Lookup
ladders.desc: Reverse a string and check if it exists in a hashmap of words.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that we need to quickly find if the reverse of a string exists among given words. Reversing can be done by slicing or iterating backwards. HashMap provides O(1) average lookup. Algorithm: To reverse string s, use s[::-1] in Python or iterate from end to start. Then check if this reversed string is a key in a pre-built hashmap that maps word to index. Time: O(k) to reverse, O(1) to lookup. Space: O(1) extra. Connection to L2: This is used in L2 to find candidate words after checking palindrome conditions. Common mistakes: Not handling empty string reversal (stays empty), or assuming case sensitivity. Real code pattern: reversed_str = s[::-1]; if reversed_str in word_map: index = word_map[reversed_str]

examples.trace: Step 1: Reverse "hello" to "olleh". Step 2: Check if "olleh" is in map keys: yes, value is 0. Return 0.
Step 1: Reverse "test" to "tset". Step 2: Check if "tset" is in map keys: no. Return -1 or None.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterate Over All Prefixes and Suffixes of a String
ladders.desc: Generate all possible prefixes and suffixes by splitting a string at every position.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that to check palindrome conditions, we need to consider every possible way to split a word into two parts. A prefix is the substring from start to split point, and suffix is from split point to end. Algorithm: For a string s of length L, iterate i from 0 to L inclusive. Prefix = s[0:i], suffix = s[i:L]. This includes empty prefix (i=0) and empty suffix (i=L). Time: O(k) to generate all splits (since we just change indices). Space: O(1) if generating on fly. Connection to L3: This provides the prefixes/suffixes that are then reversed and looked up in L3. Common mistakes: Off-by-one errors (e.g., using range(len(s)) instead of len(s)+1), or not including empty strings. Real code pattern: for i in range(len(s)+1): prefix = s[:i]; suffix = s[i:]

examples.trace: Step 1: i=0: prefix="", suffix="abc". Step 2: i=1: prefix="a", suffix="bc". Step 3: i=2: prefix="ab", suffix="c". Step 4: i=3: prefix="abc", suffix="".
Step 1: i=0: prefix="", suffix="" (only one split since length 0).

testCases.expected: prefix="", suffix="ab"; prefix="a", suffix="b"; prefix="ab", suffix=""
prefix="", suffix="abc"; prefix="a", suffix="bc"; prefix="ab", suffix="c"; prefix="abc", suffix=""
prefix="", suffix="aa"; prefix="a", suffix="a"; prefix="aa", suffix=""
prefix="", suffix="bat"; prefix="b", suffix="at"; prefix="ba", suffix="t"; prefix="bat", suffix=""

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — What is a Palindrome?
ladders.desc: Understand the definition and examples of palindromes.
ladders.explanation: A palindrome is a sequence (like a string) that reads the same forwards and backwards. Intuition: It's symmetric around its center. Examples: "racecar", "madam", "12321". Empty string and single characters are trivially palindromes. Algorithm to check: Compare first and last characters, then move inward. Step-by-step: 1) If string length is 0 or 1, it's a palindrome. 2) Set left index to 0, right to length-1. 3) While left < right: if characters at left and right are different, return false; else increment left and decrement right. 4) If loop completes, return true. Time: O(n) where n is length. Space: O(1). Connection to L4: This concept is used to check if prefixes/suffixes in L4 are palindromes. Common mistakes: Confusing palindromes with other patterns, or not considering case sensitivity. Real code pattern: def is_palindrome(s): return s == s[::-1] (simple but O(n) space) or use two-pointer as above.

examples.trace: Compare positions: 0 vs 6: 'r'='r'; 1 vs 5: 'a'='a'; 2 vs 4: 'c'='c'; 3 vs 3: stop. All match, so true.
Compare positions: 0 vs 4: 'h' != 'o', so false immediately.

approaches.name: Trie O(n * k²)
approaches.summary: Build a Trie with all words inserted in reverse order, then for each word, traverse the Trie to find matches where the remaining part of the word or the Trie node forms a palindrome. This efficiently handles prefix and suffix checks.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find All Palindrome Pairs Using Trie
ladders.desc: Given a list of unique words, find all index pairs (i, j) such that the concatenation word[i] + word[j] is a palindrome.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires finding all pairs of words that form a palindrome when concatenated. The naive O(n² * k) approach compares every pair and checks palindrome property. Our optimized approach uses a Trie data structure to achieve O(n * k²) time complexity. The core intuition is to insert all words in reverse order into a Trie. For each word, we traverse the Trie character by character. At each node, we check two conditions: 1) If we've reached the end of a word in the Trie (meaning the current word's prefix matches a reversed word exactly), we check if the remaining suffix of the current word is a palindrome. 2) If we're at a Trie node with palindrome suffixes stored, we check if the remaining part of the current word is empty (meaning we've matched the entire word). We also handle the case where a word is paired with itself by ensuring i ≠ j. The algorithm: 1) Build Trie with reversed words, storing at each node the indices of words ending there and a list of indices where the remaining suffix from that node is a palindrome. 2) For each word, traverse Trie: a) If node marks end of word and remaining suffix is palindrome, add pair. b) If word ends but Trie continues, check stored palindrome suffixes. 3) Avoid duplicates by careful indexing. Time: O(n * k²) where n is number of words, k is average length. Space: O(n * k) for Trie storage.

examples.trace: Step 1: Build Trie with reversed words. Insert reverse("bat") = "tab": root-t-a-b (store index 0 at b). Insert reverse("tab") = "bat": root-b-a-t (store index 1 at t). Insert reverse("cat") = "tac": root-t-a-c (store index 2 at c). Step 2: Process each word. For "bat": traverse chars b-a-t. At root, go to b. At b, go to a. At a, go to t. At t, it's end of word (index 1 stored). Remaining suffix of "bat" is empty (we matched all chars). Check: empty string is palindrome. Add pair [0,1]. For "tab": traverse t-a-b. At root, go to t. At t, go to a. At a, go to b. At b, end of word (index 0). Remaining suffix empty. Add pair [1,0]. For "cat": traverse c-a-t. At root, no 'c' child, stop. No pairs. Final: [[0,1],[1,0]].

Step 1: Build Trie with reversed words. Insert "dcba", "abcd", "sll", "s", "llsss". Store indices at end nodes and palindrome suffixes. Step 2: Process "abcd". Traverse a-b-c-d. Matches reversed "dcba" (index 1). Remaining suffix empty. Add [0,1]. Process "dcba". Traverse d-c-b-a. Matches reversed "abcd" (index 0). Add [1,0]. Process "lls". Traverse l-l-s. At second l, node has palindrome suffix list (from "s" stored). Check remaining "s" is palindrome? Yes, single char. Add [2,3]. Continue to s, end of word (no match). Process "s". Traverse s. At s, end of word (index 3). Check remaining empty. Also, node has palindrome suffixes from "lls" stored. For suffix "lls", check if reverse is palindrome? "sll" not palindrome, skip. Process "sssll". Traverse s-s-s-l-l. At third s, node has palindrome suffix from "lls" stored. Check remaining "ll" is palindrome? Yes. Add [2,4]. Final pairs: [[0,1],[1,0],[3,2],[2,4]].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Trie Construction with Palindrome Suffix Detection
ladders.desc: Build a Trie from reversed words while detecting and storing palindrome suffixes at each node.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the efficient palindrome pairs solution. We need to insert all words in reverse order into a Trie, but with additional information: at each node, we store which words end at that node, and crucially, we store indices of words where the remaining suffix from that node to the end of the word is a palindrome. Why? When searching for pairs, if we match part of a word with a reversed word in the Trie, the remaining part must be a palindrome to form a valid pair. By precomputing these palindrome suffixes during insertion, we avoid repeated palindrome checks during search. Algorithm: 1) Define Trie node with children map, end_index (list of word indices ending here), and palindrome_suffixes (list of word indices where suffix from this node is palindrome). 2) For each word index i: a) Reverse the word. b) Traverse Trie with reversed word. c) At each node j (including root), check if the remaining suffix (from position j to end) is a palindrome. If yes, add i to node.palindrome_suffixes. d) At final node, add i to end_index. Time: O(n * k²) for n words of average length k (due to palindrome checks). Space: O(n * k). Connection to L0: This preprocessed Trie enables O(k) search per word instead of O(k²).

examples.trace: Step 1: Process "bat" (index 0). Reverse = "tab". Start at root. Char 't': create node if not exists. Check remaining "ab" is palindrome? 'a' vs 'b' no. Move to t node. Char 'a': create node. Check remaining "b" is palindrome? single char yes. Add index 0 to t node's palindrome_suffixes. Move to a node. Char 'b': create node. Check remaining "" is palindrome? empty yes. Add index 0 to a node's palindrome_suffixes. At b node: mark end of word, add index 0 to end_index. Step 2: Process "tab" (index 1). Reverse = "bat". Root->b: create. Check "at" palindrome? no. b->a: create. Check "t" palindrome? yes. Add index 1 to b node's palindrome_suffixes. a->t: create. Check "" palindrome? yes. Add index 1 to a node's palindrome_suffixes. t node: mark end, add index 1 to end_index.

Step 1: "a" (index 0). Reverse = "a". Root->a: create. Check remaining "" palindrome? yes. Add index 0 to root's palindrome_suffixes. At a node: mark end, add index 0 to end_index. Step 2: "aa" (index 1). Reverse = "aa". Root->a: exists. Check remaining "a" palindrome? yes. Add index 1 to root's palindrome_suffixes. Move to a node. Check remaining "" palindrome? yes. Add index 1 to a node's palindrome_suffixes. Create new a node? No, but we need to mark end of "aa". Actually, "aa" has length 2, so we traverse: root->a (first char), then a->a (second char). At second a node: mark end, add index 1 to end_index.

testCases.expected: Trie with root having palindrome_suffixes for both words
Trie storing indices at appropriate nodes
Trie with three branches from root

ladders.title: Core Logic — Matching Words Against Reverse Trie with Palindrome Check
ladders.desc: For a given word, traverse the reverse Trie to find pairs where concatenation forms a palindrome.
ladders.explanation: This core logic implements the search phase of the algorithm. Given a word and a Trie built from reversed words, we traverse the Trie character by character from the word's start. At each node during traversal, we perform two critical checks: 1) If the current Trie node marks the end of a reversed word (i.e., has end_index), then we've matched the entire reversed word against a prefix of our current word. We need to check if the remaining suffix of the current word is a palindrome. If yes, we found a pair (current_word_index, reversed_word_index). 2) If we exhaust the current word (i.e., reach its end), then we've matched the entire current word against a prefix of some reversed word. We need to check all words stored in the current node's palindrome_suffixes list. These are words where the suffix from this node is a palindrome, meaning when concatenated, they form a palindrome. We must avoid self-pairing (i ≠ j). The intuition: By traversing the Trie with the original word while the Trie contains reversed words, we're effectively checking all possible concatenations where the current word comes first. The palindrome check on the remaining part ensures the combined string is symmetric. Time per word: O(k²) in worst case due to palindrome checks. Space: O(1) extra. Connection to L1: Uses the Trie built in L1 to efficiently find matches.

examples.trace: Trie has reverse words: "tab" (index 0), "bat" (index 1). Traverse "bat": char 'b' -> go to b node in Trie. Char 'a' -> go to a node. Char 't' -> go to t node. At t node, end_index contains [1] (reversed "bat" corresponds to original index 1). Remaining suffix of "bat" is empty (we matched all chars). Check palindrome: empty is palindrome. Add pair (current_index=0, 1). Also, at each node, check palindrome_suffixes: none relevant here.

Trie has reverse words: "s" (index 3), "sll" (index 2), etc. Traverse "lls": char 'l' -> go to l node. Check end_index? none. Check palindrome_suffixes? none. Char 'l' -> go to l node (second level). Check end_index? none. Check palindrome_suffixes? contains index 3 (from "s" where suffix "" is palindrome). Remaining suffix of "lls" is "s". Check if "s" is palindrome? Yes, single char. Add pair (2,3). Continue to 's' -> go to s node. end_index contains [2]? Actually reverse "sll" has index 2. Remaining suffix empty. Add pair? But we already processed? Need to avoid duplicate. Typically we add only when i ≠ j.

ladders.title: Building Block — Efficient Palindrome Checking for Substrings
ladders.desc: Determine if a substring is a palindrome in O(1) time after preprocessing.
ladders.explanation: A critical optimization in the Trie approach is checking if the remaining part of a word is a palindrome. Doing this naively for each check would cost O(k) time, leading to O(n * k³) overall. We can preprocess each word to answer palindrome queries for any substring in O(1) time. The intuition: Use dynamic programming or rolling hashes. For DP, create a 2D table where dp[i][j] indicates if substring from i to j is palindrome. Fill it using the recurrence: dp[i][j] = (s[i] == s[j]) and (j - i < 2 or dp[i+1][j-1]). This takes O(k²) time per word. Alternatively, use two rolling hashes (forward and backward) to check equality in O(1). Algorithm: 1) For each word, compute prefix hashes for forward and reverse directions. 2) To check if substring [l, r] is palindrome, compare forward hash from l to r with reverse hash from transformed indices. 3) Handle base cases like empty string (always palindrome) and single character (always palindrome). Time: O(k) preprocessing per word, O(1) query. Space: O(k) for storage. Connection to L2: This enables fast checks during Trie traversal without blowing up time complexity.

examples.trace: Preprocess: Compute hashes. Forward hash: h(0,6) = hash("racecar"). Reverse hash: h_rev(0,6) = hash("racecar" reversed) = hash("racecar"). Since they're equal, palindrome. Or DP: dp[0][6] = (s[0]=='r' == s[6]=='r') and dp[1][5] which is true.

Preprocess hashes. Forward hash(1,3) = hash("bcb"). Reverse: need indices. For reverse string "abcba", substring [1,3] in original corresponds to reverse indices? Better use DP: dp[1][3] = (s[1]=='b' == s[3]=='b') and (3-1<2 or dp[2][2]) which is true since dp[2][2] is true (single char).

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Trie Insertion and Search
ladders.desc: Implement fundamental Trie operations: insert a string and search for a string.
ladders.explanation: A Trie (prefix tree) is a tree-like data structure where each node represents a character. The path from root to a node spells out a prefix of inserted strings. Basic operations: Insert: Start at root, for each character in the string, traverse to the corresponding child node (create if not exists). After processing all characters, mark the node as an end node (often storing the inserted word or index). Search: Start at root, traverse characters; if at any point the required child is missing, return false. If all characters are traversed and the current node is an end node, return true (exact match). For prefix search, we don't need the end node check. Time: O(L) for both operations, where L is string length. Space: O(N * L) in worst case for N strings. Intuition: Tries allow efficient prefix-based operations, which is why they're perfect for our palindrome pairs problem—we need to match prefixes of original words with reversed words. Connection to L3: In L3, we extend insertion to store additional info (palindrome suffixes), but the core traversal remains the same.

examples.trace: Step 1: Insert "cat". Root->c (create), c->a, a->t. Mark t node as end. Step 2: Insert "car". Root->c (exists), c->a (exists), a->r (create). Mark r node as end. Step 3: Search "cat". Root->c, c->a, a->t. At t node, check end marker: true. Return true.
Insert "bat": root->b, b->a, a->t, mark end. Search "ball": root->b (exists), b->a (exists), a->l (does not exist, since only 't' child). Return false.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Palindrome Definition and Basic Check
ladders.desc: Understand what a palindrome is and how to check if a string is a palindrome.
ladders.explanation: A palindrome is a string that reads the same forwards and backwards. Examples: "racecar", "madam", "a", "" (empty string). To check if a string is a palindrome, compare characters from both ends moving inward. Algorithm: 1) Initialize two pointers: left at start (index 0), right at end (index length-1). 2) While left < right: if s[left] != s[right], return false. Else, increment left, decrement right. 3) If loop completes, return true. For empty string, by convention it's considered a palindrome. Time: O(n) where n is string length. Space: O(1). Intuition: Palindromes are symmetric around the center. This property is crucial for our problem because when concatenating two words, the combined string must be symmetric. Connection to L4: In L4, Tries help find matching prefixes/suffixes, but the palindrome check ensures the overall concatenation is valid.

examples.trace: left=0 ('r'), right=6 ('r'): equal. left=1 ('a'), right=5 ('a'): equal. left=2 ('c'), right=4 ('c'): equal. left=3 ('e'), right=3: loop ends (left not < right). Return true.
left=0 ('h'), right=4 ('o'): not equal. Return false immediately.

approaches.name: Optimized Trie with Caching O(n * k)
approaches.summary: Enhance the Trie approach by caching palindrome information at each Trie node to avoid redundant checks, reducing the time complexity by precomputing which suffixes are palindromes. This is the most efficient Trie-based solution.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find All Palindrome Pairs Using Optimized Trie with Caching
ladders.desc: Complete solution to find all index pairs (i, j) where words[i] + words[j] is a palindrome using Trie with palindrome suffix caching.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete implementation of the optimized Trie approach for finding palindrome pairs. The intuition is that by building a Trie of all words and their reversed versions, we can efficiently check for palindrome pairs by traversing the Trie while maintaining palindrome information at each node. The key optimization is caching which suffixes of each word are palindromes at the Trie nodes, avoiding redundant palindrome checks during traversal. The algorithm works by first inserting all words into a Trie, storing the word index at terminal nodes and maintaining a list of indices for words whose remaining suffix from that node is a palindrome. Then, for each word, we search for its palindrome pairs by traversing the Trie with the word's characters while checking three conditions: 1) When we reach a terminal node where the other word ends, if the remaining part of our current word is a palindrome, we have a pair. 2) When our current word ends at a non-terminal node, we check all words in that node's palindrome list. 3) We also need to handle the case where one word is a prefix of another. The time complexity is O(n * k) where n is number of words and k is average word length, as each character is processed during insertion and search. Space complexity is O(n * k) for the Trie structure. This approach avoids the O(k²) palindrome checks of brute force by precomputing palindrome information in the Trie structure.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Build Trie with all words. Insert "bat" (index 0): Insert b->a->t, mark node t as terminal with index 0. Check suffixes: at t, remaining suffix "" is palindrome, add index 0 to palindrome list at node t. Insert "tab" (index 1): Insert t->a->b, mark node b as terminal with index 1. Check suffixes: at b, remaining suffix "" is palindrome, add index 1 to palindrome list at node b. Insert "cat" (index 2): Insert c->a->t, mark node t as terminal with index 2. Check suffixes: at t, remaining suffix "" is palindrome, add index 2 to palindrome list at node t.
Step 2: Search for palindrome pairs. For word "bat" (index 0): Traverse Trie with "bat": b->a->t. At node t: It's terminal with index 0 (same word, skip). Check if remaining part of "bat" is empty (yes). No other indices in palindrome list at node t. For word "tab" (index 1): Traverse Trie with "tab": t->a->b. At node b: It's terminal with index 1 (same word, skip). Check if remaining part of "tab" is empty (yes). No other indices in palindrome list at node b. For word "cat" (index 2): Traverse Trie with "cat": c->a->t. At node t: It's terminal with index 2 (same word, skip). Check if remaining part of "cat" is empty (yes). No other indices in palindrome list at node t.
Step 3: Search reversed words. For word "bat" (index 0): Search reversed "tab" in Trie: t->a->b. At node b: It's terminal with index 1. Check if remaining part of "bat" is empty (yes). Add pair [0, 1]. For word "tab" (index 1): Search reversed "bat" in Trie: b->a->t. At node t: It's terminal with index 0. Check if remaining part of "tab" is empty (yes). Add pair [1, 0]. For word "cat" (index 2): Search reversed "tac" in Trie: t->a->c not found. Final result: [[0, 1], [1, 0]].

Step 1: Build Trie. Insert all 5 words with indices 0-4, storing palindrome suffix information.
Step 2: Search for pairs. For "abcd" (index 0): Search reversed "dcba" in Trie: d->c->b->a reaches terminal node with index 1, remaining part empty → add [0, 1]. For "dcba" (index 1): Search reversed "abcd" in Trie: a->b->c->d reaches terminal node with index 0, remaining part empty → add [1, 0]. For "lls" (index 2): Search reversed "sll" in Trie: s->l->l. At second l node: Not terminal but has palindrome list containing index 3 (word "s" whose remaining suffix "ll" is palindrome). Check remaining part of "lls" is "" (empty) → add [2, 3]. Also search "lls" itself: l->l->s reaches terminal node with index 2 (skip). For "s" (index 3): Search reversed "s" in Trie: s reaches terminal node with index 3 (skip). Also traverse with "s": At node s: terminal with index 3, check palindrome list containing index 2 (word "lls" whose remaining suffix "" is palindrome) → add [3, 2]. For "sssll" (index 4): Search reversed "llsss" in Trie: l->l->s->s->s. At third s node: terminal with index 4 (skip). Also search "sssll" itself: s->s->s->l->l reaches node l (second l): Not terminal but has palindrome list containing index 2 (word "lls" whose remaining suffix "sss" is palindrome? Actually need to check: For word "lls" at this node, remaining suffix would be "sss" which is palindrome). Check remaining part of "sssll" is "" → add [4, 2]. Final result: [[0, 1], [1, 0], [3, 2], [2, 4]].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Trie Search with Palindrome Checking
ladders.desc: Search function that traverses the Trie with a word to find all palindrome pairs involving that word.
ladders.explanation: This is the core search routine that finds palindrome pairs for a given word using the pre-built Trie with palindrome caching. The intuition is that by traversing the Trie with either the word or its reverse, we can efficiently identify pairs where concatenation forms a palindrome. The algorithm takes a word and its index, then performs two searches: one with the word itself and one with its reverse. During traversal, at each node we check three conditions: 1) If the current node is a terminal node (marks the end of another word), we check if the remaining suffix of our current word is a palindrome. If yes, we found a pair (current_index, node_word_index). 2) If our current word ends at this node (we've consumed all characters), we check all word indices in the node's palindrome list. These are words that have a palindrome suffix from this node onward. 3) We need to avoid pairing a word with itself. The search with the word's reverse handles cases where one word is the reverse of another. The time complexity for searching one word is O(k + p) where k is word length and p is number of palindrome indices encountered. Space complexity is O(1) excluding the Trie. This subroutine is called for each word in the main algorithm, making it the performance-critical component.

examples.trace: Step 1: Search with word "bat": Traverse b->a->t. At node t: It's terminal with index 0 (same word, skip). Check if remaining part of "bat" is empty (yes). No other indices in palindrome list. Step 2: Search with reversed word "tab": Traverse t->a->b. At node b: It's terminal with index 1. Check if remaining part of "bat" is empty (yes). Add pair [0, 1]. Step 3: Return [[0, 1]].

Step 1: Search with word "lls": Traverse l->l->s. At node s: Not terminal. Our word ends here (consumed all characters). Check palindrome list at node s: Contains index 1 (word "s" whose remaining suffix "ll" is palindrome). Add pair [0, 1]. Step 2: Search with reversed word "sll": Traverse s->l->l. At second l node: Not terminal. Check if remaining part of "lls" is empty (yes). Palindrome list at this node is empty. Step 3: Return [[0, 1]].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Caching Palindrome Suffixes in Trie Nodes
ladders.desc: Precomputing and storing which word suffixes are palindromes at each Trie node to avoid redundant checks.
ladders.explanation: This is the central optimization technique that makes the Trie approach efficient. The intuition is that during Trie insertion, we can precompute for each node which words have a palindrome suffix starting from that node. This information allows us to quickly identify palindrome pairs during search without repeatedly checking substrings for palindrome properties. The algorithm works as follows: When inserting a word into the Trie, at each node we reach (including the terminal node), we check if the remaining suffix of the word (from the next character to the end) is a palindrome. If it is, we add the word's index to a list stored at that node. For example, when inserting "lls", at the root node we check if "lls" is palindrome (no). At the first 'l' node, check if "ls" is palindrome (no). At the second 'l' node, check if "s" is palindrome (yes) - so we add index of "lls" to palindrome list at the second 'l' node. At the terminal 's' node, check if "" is palindrome (yes) - add index to palindrome list at 's' node. This caching means that during search, when we reach a node, we can immediately know all words that when concatenated with our current word might form a palindrome. The time complexity for this precomputation is O(n * k²) in naive implementation, but can be optimized to O(n * k) using two-pointer palindrome checking from both ends. Space complexity is O(n * k) for storing the lists.

examples.output: Trie with palindrome lists: root: [], l (first): [], l (second): [0], s (terminal): [0]
examples.trace:
Step 1: Start at root. Remaining suffix: "lls". Check if "lls" is palindrome: l==s? No. Don't add index to root.
Step 2: Move to 'l' node (first). Remaining suffix: "ls". Check if "ls" is palindrome: l==s? No. Don't add index to first 'l' node.
Step 3: Move to 'l' node (second). Remaining suffix: "s". Check if "s" is palindrome: Single character, yes. Add index 0 to palindrome list at second 'l' node.
Step 4: Move to 's' node (terminal). Remaining suffix: "". Check if "" is palindrome: Empty string, yes. Add index 0 to palindrome list at 's' node.
Step 5: Mark 's' node as terminal with index 0.

examples.input: Insert word "s" (index 1) into Trie from previous example
examples.trace:
Step 1: Start at root. Remaining suffix: "s". Check if "s" is palindrome: Yes. Add index 1 to palindrome list at root.
Step 2: Move to 's' node (existing from "lls"). Remaining suffix: "". Check if "" is palindrome: Yes. Add index 1 to palindrome list at 's' node.
Step 3: Mark 's' node as terminal with index 1 (in addition to existing index 0).

testCases.expected: root palindrome list: [0], terminal 'a' palindrome list: [0]

ladders.title: Building Block — Trie Data Structure for String Storage
ladders.desc: Implementing a Trie (prefix tree) to store a collection of strings with efficient prefix searching.
ladders.explanation: A Trie is a tree-like data structure used to store strings where each node represents a character and paths from root to leaves represent strings. The intuition is that strings sharing common prefixes share nodes in the Trie, allowing efficient prefix-based operations. For this problem, we need a Trie that supports: 1) Insertion of a word with its index, 2) Terminal nodes marking the end of a word, 3) Storage of word indices at nodes, 4) A list at each node to store indices of words with palindrome suffixes. The algorithm for insertion: Start at root, for each character in the word, create child node if it doesn't exist, move to that node. After processing all characters, mark the node as terminal and store the word index. For search: Start at root, for each character in the search string, if child exists, move to it; otherwise return not found. The time complexity for insertion and search of one word is O(k) where k is word length. Space complexity is O(n * k) in worst case where no prefixes are shared. This Trie forms the foundation for the optimized solution by organizing words in a way that allows efficient palindrome pair discovery.

examples.output: Trie structure: root->b->a->t (terminal, index 0); root->t->a->b (terminal, index 1)
examples.trace:
Step 1: Insert "bat" (index 0). Start at root. Character 'b': Create child node 'b'. Move to 'b'. Character 'a': Create child node 'a' under 'b'. Move to 'a'. Character 't': Create child node 't' under 'a'. Move to 't'. Mark node 't' as terminal with index 0.
Step 2: Insert "tab" (index 1). Start at root. Character 't': Create child node 't'. Move to 't'. Character 'a': Create child node 'a' under 't'. Move to 'a'. Character 'b': Create child node 'b' under 'a'. Move to 'b'. Mark node 'b' as terminal with index 1.

examples.input: Search for "bat" in the above Trie
examples.output: Found at terminal node with index 0
examples.trace: Step 1: Start at root. Character 'b': Child 'b' exists. Move to 'b'. Character 'a': Child 'a' exists under 'b'. Move to 'a'. Character 't': Child 't' exists under 'a'. Move to 't'. Node 't' is terminal with index 0. Return found.

testCases.expected: Trie with root->a (terminal, index 0) and root->b (terminal, index 1)
Trie: root->a->b (terminal, index 0) and root->a->c (terminal, index 1)
Trie: root->c->a->t (terminal, index 0) and root->c->a->r (terminal, index 1)
Trie: root->t->e->s->t (terminal, index 0) and continuing ->i->n->g (terminal, index 1)

testCases.input: Search "a" in Trie from test case 1
Search "ab" in Trie from test case 3
Search "ac" in Trie from test case 3
Search "cab" in Trie from test case 4
Search "test" in Trie from test case 5

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Checking if a String is a Palindrome
ladders.desc: Determining whether a given string reads the same forwards and backwards.
ladders.explanation: A palindrome is a string that reads the same forward and backward. The intuition for checking is to compare characters from both ends moving toward the center. The algorithm uses two pointers: one starting at the beginning (left) and one at the end (right). While left < right, compare characters at these positions. If any pair doesn't match, the string is not a palindrome. If all pairs match, it is a palindrome. For empty string, it's considered a palindrome. For single character string, it's always a palindrome. The time complexity is O(k) where k is string length, as we examine each character at most once. Space complexity is O(1) as we only use pointers. This operation is fundamental because we need to check palindrome properties frequently in the main algorithm: both during Trie insertion (to cache palindrome suffixes) and during search (to check remaining parts of words).

examples.trace:
Step 1: left = 0 ('r'), right = 6 ('r'). Compare: 'r' == 'r' ✓
Step 2: left = 1 ('a'), right = 5 ('a'). Compare: 'a' == 'a' ✓
Step 3: left = 2 ('c'), right = 4 ('c'). Compare: 'c' == 'c' ✓
Step 4: left = 3 ('e'), right = 3 ('e'). left >= right, stop. All comparisons matched, so palindrome.

Step 1: left = 0 ('h'), right = 4 ('o'). Compare: 'h' == 'o' ✗
Step 2: Mismatch found, return false immediately.

ladders.title: L5: Concept Foundation — String Manipulation and Indexing
ladders.desc: Basic operations on strings: accessing characters, slicing, and understanding indices.
ladders.explanation: Strings are sequences of characters. In programming, we typically access characters using zero-based indexing: the first character is at index 0, second at index 1, etc. String slicing allows extracting substrings: s[start:end] gives characters from index start to end-1. If start is omitted, it defaults to 0 (beginning). If end is omitted, it defaults to length (end). Understanding these operations is crucial because the palindrome pairs problem involves examining substrings of words. For example, when checking if a string is palindrome, we need to compare characters from both ends. When caching palindrome suffixes in the Trie, we need to examine remaining parts of strings. The intuition is that strings can be thought of as arrays of characters, and we can navigate them using indices. Common operations include: getting length (len(s)), accessing character at position i (s[i]), checking if index is valid (0 <= i < len(s)), and iterating through characters. These form the building blocks for all string algorithms.

examples.input: String s = "hello", access characters and slices
examples.trace:
String "hello" has indices: 0='h', 1='e', 2='l', 3='l', 4='o'. Length = 5.
s[0]: Character at index 0 = 'h'.
s[1:4]: Slice from index 1 to 3 (end-1) = indices 1,2,3 = 'e','l','l' = "ell".
s[:3]: Slice from start to index 2 = indices 0,1,2 = 'h','e','l' = "hel".
s[2:]: Slice from index 2 to end = indices 2,3,4 = 'l','l','o' = "llo".

examples.input: String s = "racecar", demonstrate palindrome checking indices
examples.output: Comparing s[0] with s[6], s[1] with s[5], etc.
examples.trace:
String "racecar" has length 7, indices 0-6.
For palindrome check:
Compare s[0]='r' with s[6]='r': equal ✓
Compare s[1]='a' with s[5]='a': equal ✓
Compare s[2]='c' with s[4]='c': equal ✓
Compare s[3]='e' with s[3]='e': equal ✓ (middle character)
All comparisons equal, so palindrome.
